issue_title: [build system] hanging docker containers after build fails or aborts
issue_body:
### System information
- **Linux Ubuntu 16.04**:
- **Ray installed from source for CI builds**:

### Describe the problem
When a Ray build ends prematurely, and after test/jenkins_tests/multi_node_docker_test.py runs, it leaves behind docker containers that take ~500G of disk space.

### Source code / logs
in test/jenkins_tests/multi_node_docker_test.py:
```
        command = (["docker", "run", "-d"] + mem_arg + shm_arg + volume_arg +
                   [docker_image, "ray", "start", "--head", "--block",
                    "--redis-port=6379",
                    "--num-redis-shards={}".format(num_redis_shards),
                    "--num-cpus={}".format(num_cpus),
                    "--num-gpus={}".format(num_gpus),
                    "--no-ui"])
        print("Starting head node with command:{}".format(command))

        proc = subprocess.Popen(command,
                                stdout=subprocess.PIPE, stderr=subprocess.PIPE)
```

after speaking w/dan crankshaw about his docker testing approach, he strongly suggests the following (which i agree with):

1.  add a tag/label to each and every docker container that is launched
2.  add a final step to your tests that goes through this list of tags and explicitly kills these containers
3. set up all of your builds to be limited to one executing on a worker at any time.  this will keep builds from stomping on other ray containers.

issue_title: Random variation in traffic behavior (aggressiveness / passiveness)
Incompatibility with Python 3 versions lower than 3.5
issue_body:
It appears that the library is incompatible with Python versions lower than 3.5, despite claiming to be [fully Python 3 compatible](https://github.com/chainside/btcpy/blob/master/setup.py#L26).

The issue stems from the following idiom mainly:

```python
 m, *pubkeys, n = args
```

Unpacking rules were much stricter prior to Python 3.5 and did not allow this type of argument unpacking where a single argument follows star arguments.

In order for the library to be compatible with Python 3 versions lower than 3.5, it'd have to use the following idiom instead:

```python
 m, n, *pubkeys = args
```

This appears to happen in quite a few places. Python 3.4 compatibility would be nice because that version of Python 3 often comes preinstalled with many systems.

Reference: https://www.python.org/dev/peps/pep-0448/

A `DatePicker` really is about selecting a date and should probably yield a `datetime.date` value. It it implemented by a [`<input type=date>`](https://www.w3.org/TR/html5/forms.html#date-state-(type=date)) which is supposed to convey a [date](https://www.w3.org/TR/html5/infrastructure.html#concept-date). 

Currently `DatePicker` yields a `datetime.datetime`:

~~~
@widgets.interact(x=widgets.DatePicker())
def foo(x):
    return x
=> datetime.datetime(2017, 5, 19, 0, 0)
~~~

It would be nice to be able to have `DatePicker` work on `datetime.date` (as an option ?).

issue_title: Should send_all automatically do wait_send_all_might_not_block after sending the data?
issue_body:
Currently `send_all` blocks until the kernel accepts responsibility for the data, and then immediately returns. I'm wondering if it should instead block until the kernel has not only accepted responsibility, but also until the the kernel send buffer has some space in it (i.e., the socket becomes writable again).

Pros:

* It would make `TCP_NOTSENT_LOWAT` automatically work on MacOS. Currently it only works if you explicitly call `wait_send_all_might_not_block`, because the `TCP_NOTSENT_LOWAT` is only applied to `select`/`kqueue` type writability checks, *not* to `send` writability (discussion here: #76). This would also let us normalize `TCP_NOTSENT_LOWAT` semantics across MacOS and Linux (which *does* apply `TCP_NOTSENT_LOWAT` limits to `send`) -- in both cases `send_all` would wait to return until after the low water mark was reached. It would also open the door to a potential optimization on Linux: when a large buffer is passed to `send_all`, we could temporarily disable `TCP_NOTSENT_LOWAT` (so that the kernel accepts it in one big chunk instead of making us dribble it into the send buffer over multiple calls), and then re-enable it, and then wait for writability.

* It would allow us to remove `wait_send_all_might_not_block`, which adds significant surface area to the `Stream` API (it would go from 4 methods → 3 methods, 25% smaller, and testing this method in particular adds significant complications).

* It's generally consistent with the idea that `send_all` blocks until the data is sent -- not only do we hand it off to the kernel, we wait for the kernel to make some progress. (I guess this isn't so much a pro, as a counter to the possible "con" that `send_all` waiting is surprising.)

* It automatically gives better results for latency-sensitive applications, in particular those where you get better results when waiting as late as possible before committing to what you want to send (e.g. screen-sharing). These apps are why `wait_send_all_might_not_block` exists, but currently it's not clear they'll actually benefit because this is a bit obscure and you have to explicitly set up your code to use it.

Cons:

* Maybe it will kill throughput on super high bandwidth applications, or something? (Stuff like an echo server benchmark, but possibly also [stuff like Antoine's worrying about here](https://mail.python.org/pipermail/async-sig/2017-October/000392.html). Would want to check this. OTOH maybe it's fine because we'd end up replacing a loop like:

```python
while True:
    await wait_writable(socket)
    socket.send(...)
```

with a loop like:

```python
while True:
    socket.send(...)
    await wait_writable(socket)
```

issue_title: Split Result off into a standalone library?
issue_body:
`Result` and its subclasses are a self-contained reusable abstraction. Which has made it awkward to figure out where to put them: in `trio` feels weird b/c they're not needed at all for using trio's main APIs, but `trio.hazmat` also feels weird because there's absolutely nothing hazardous about them, and in fact they are sometimes useful in ordinary code that's not using anything else in `hazmat`. (I guess this is related to #476.)

The most natural solution is perhaps to split them off into their own library that trio would depend on. Unfortunately `result` is [taken on PyPI](https://pypi.org/project/result/), though I guess [`results` is free](https://pypi.org/project/results/). Or I guess [`outcome` is free](https://pypi.org/project/outcome/).

[this was originally #79, but that thread got taken over by the discussion of whether we can provide better diagnostics on missing `await`, so I'm moving the umbrella issue here]

This is currently very rough and needs expanding into something we can actually bring to the Python core team, but so I don't forget:

- `__iterclose__` ([PEP 533](https://www.python.org/dev/peps/pep-0533/))

- can we do anything about how easy it is to forget an await? In retrospect async functions shouldn't implement `__call__` IMO...  but probably too late to fix that. Still, it kinda sucks that one of first things in our tutorial is a [giant warning](https://trio.readthedocs.io/en/latest/tutorial.html#warning-don-t-forget-that-await) about this wart, so worth at least checking if anyone has any ideas... [see #79 for discussion]

- possibly Result should actually be builtin? I think it would actually really simplify the generator API and implementation. (in particular, unifying send and throw could dramatically simplify the implementation of yield from while fixing some of the weird `throw` bugs we have to work around)

- speaking of which, straightforward bugs that affect us:
  - ~~[issue29600](https://bugs.python.org/issue29600) (this is the worst; it actually blocks us from implementing some useful API, like an async version of `MultiError.catch`)~~ ([now fixed](https://github.com/python/cpython/pull/644), thanks @1st1!)
  - [issue29590](https://bugs.python.org/issue29590)
  - [issue29587](https://bugs.python.org/issue29587)
  - [issue29728](https://bugs.python.org/issue29728)
  - [issue29515](https://bugs.python.org/issue29515)

- better ergonomics for MultiErrors (catching, printing, rethrowing...). There are a number of pieces to this; I'm not sure all of them. But:
  - no brainer: making traceback objects instantiable or mutable (both we and jinja2 are carrying disgusting code to work around this)
  - would be nice: attaching attributes to tracebacks (probably: subclassing them)
    - one use case here is to hide/de-emphasize parts of the traceback that are in trio's guts; I think Nick had a similar use case for wanting a way to hide tracebacks inside the import machinery guts?
  - better control over implicit exception chaining. here's an example where implicit exception chaining corrupts our exception reporting and there's currently nothing we can do about it:
    ```python
    v = ValueError()
    v.__context__ = KeyError()
    def discard_NameError(exc):
        if isinstance(exc, NameError):
            return None
        return exc
    with pytest.raises(ValueError) as excinfo:
        with MultiError.catch(discard_NameError):
            raise MultiError([v, NameError()])
    assert isinstance(excinfo.value.__context__, KeyError)
    assert not excinfo.value.__suppress_context__
    ```
    because Python *will* overwrite the ValueError's `__context__` in the catch's `__exit__`, even though it's already set. There's no way to stop it.

  - better hooks to control exception printing? This is the biggie – overriding `sys.excepthook` is really tacky, and has major limitations (e.g. ipython and pytest have their own exception printing code). But it's also the vaguest, because I'm not sure what this would look like

- better support for KI management (in particular for `__(a)exit__` blocks, with their currently unfixable race condition). This is complicated and I'm not 100% sure I understand it, but, some notes:
  - for sync context managers, `SETUP_WITH` atomically calls `__enter__` + sets up the finally block
  - for async context managers, the async setup stuff is split over multiple bytecodes, so we lose this guarantee -- it's possible for a KI to arrive in between calling `__aenter__` and the `SETUP_ASYNC_WITH` that pushes the finally block
  - for sync context managers, `WITH_CLEANUP_START` atomically calls `__exit__`
  - for async context managers, there again are multiple bytecodes (`WITH_CLEANUP_START` calls `__aenter__` (i.e., instantiates the coroutine object), then `GET_AWAITABLE` calls `__await__`, then `LOAD_CONST` to get the `None` to use as the initial `send`, then `YIELD_FROM` to actually run the `__aenter__` body
  - and, of course, even once we enter the `__(a)exit__`, there's currently no way for that entry to atomically enable KI protection.
  - so, proposals, I think?:
    - make entering an `__aexit__` a single bytecode, or otherwise atomic WRT exception delivery
    - add a field to the stack frame that points to the function object (if any). This avoids circular references (unlike putting it on the code object), would make entry/exit atomic, and also much more elegant than the current locals hack, would help other projects like pytest with it's `__tracebackhide__` hack, and would enable [better introspection in general](https://stackoverflow.com/questions/14817788/python-traceback-with-module-names) (right now you can't even print `__qualname__` in tracebacks!)

Note, an interesting thing in ceval.c:
```c
       if (_Py_OPCODE(*next_instr) == SETUP_FINALLY) {
           /* Make the last opcode before
              a try: finally: block uninterruptible. */
           goto fast_next_opcode;
       }
```
I'm not sure what this really accomplishes, but it suggests that someone has at least thought about some of these issues at some point :-).

issue_title: Provide an API that does WaitForSingleObject on Windows
issue_body:
As noted in #52, there are some events on Windows that can only be detected by calling one of the `WaitFor` family of functions (`WaitForSingleObject`, `WaitForSingleObjectEx`, `WaitForMultipleObjects`, etc. etc.). Some examples include child process exit (required for #4), and detecting console input (required for #174).

To resolve this issue, we should provide a `trio.hazmat.WaitForSingleObject` function that takes a `HANDLE`, and waits for it, and is cancellable.

The most straightforward way to do this is to allocate a thread to sit in `WaitForSingleObjectEx`, which can do an "alertable wait", which means that it's possible to cause the function to wake up early by calling `QueueUserAPC`. So we would use `QueueUserAPC` from inside our abort callback to implement cancellation. There are some more notes in `_io_windows.py`.

issue_title: Search route, maybe other on JSONModelObject throws AttributeError
issue_body:
Currently, calling methods on JSONModelObjects that require arguments other than all_ids to return successfully causes an AttributeError to be raised.

Triggering code:
```python
x.repositories(2).search # should be JSONRelation, throws AttributeError
```

Cause:
Currently, __getattribute__ on jsonmodels does a call with all_ids, eagerly at time of object creation.  This is fine (if extra work) for index routes.  It fails dramatically for search.

Honestly, I'm not sure what the Right Thing is to do.  There isn't a generic way to handle this "properly" (i.e. fails in __getattribute__ with an error saying that there's no route, because HEAD ALSO errors out for search.  I'm hesitant to just say "if you call something that's not on the object, you get a relation and then the relation fails if it wasn't real" because that moves the place failures happen potentially several steps down the road; you don't find out it's bad until you try to iterate through the relation.

Are all flag names assumed to be globally unique within an Abseil application? For example, assume I have a main application with a `debug` flag, that imports a library that also supplies a `debug` flag that is unrelated to the one in the main application. It would be awesome if I could provide: `__main__.debug=false webapp.lib.debug=true`as my command line and have both flags parsed correctly.

Do you think this is a worthwhile use case to support? It would be great for large applications where your libraries and applications are developed independently, and probably by different teams.

I could force namespaced flags by requiring each name to be defined as `__name__ + '.' + name`, but  it would be awesome if the library supported it out of the box.

issue_title: S3 content-type download when upload content-type was specified is being ignored
issue_body:
According to S3 documentation (https://docs.aws.amazon.com/AmazonS3/latest/API/RESTObjectPUT.html) the content-type set when downloading a file from S3 is supposed to be:
1.  The same content-type provided when uploading the file;
2.  ``binary/octet-stream`` otherwise.

Using this test script (from @TheJefe) we can see that is not what is actually happening. 

```python
import boto3
import os
from botocore.config import Config
import requests

bucket_name = 'test'
filepath = 'blah.txt'
key = 'blah.txt'
host = 'localhost'
port = 4572

s3 = boto3.resource(
    's3',
    endpoint_url = 'http://{}:{}'.format(host, port)
)

s3.create_bucket(Bucket=bucket_name)
bucket = s3.Bucket(bucket_name)

bucket.upload_file(filepath, key, ExtraArgs={"ACL": 'public-read', "ContentType": 'text/html'})
url = s3.meta.client.generate_presigned_url('get_object', ExpiresIn=0, Params={'Bucket': bucket_name, 'Key': key})
url = url.split('?')[0]
response = requests.get(url)
print 'Response content-type for text/html: ' + response.headers['Content-Type']

bucket.delete_objects(Delete={'Objects': [{'Key': key},],})

bucket.upload_file(filepath, key, ExtraArgs={"ACL": 'public-read'3.create_bucket(Bucket='test-bucket'))
url = s3.meta.client.generate_presigned_url('get_object', ExpiresIn=0, Params={'Bucket': bucket_name, 'Key': key})
url = url.split('?')[0]
response = requests.get(url)
print 'Response content-type for binary/octet-stream: ' + response.headers['Content-Type']
```

After #565 things change a little bit and all download content-types are set to ``binary/octet-stream``, independent from the provided content-type in the upload step. Before that, version 0.8.4, the content-type seems to be inferred from the uploaded archive when it wasn't provided in the upload step, otherwise it is correct. The result from the previous script agaisnt 0.8.4 is the following:
```text
Response content-type for text/html: text/html
Response content-type for binary/octet-stream: text/html; charset=utf-8
```

#126 removes the `check_existing` option from `download()` and `download_all()`, as it is quite unrelated to the main purpose of these functions. The main use case for that feature was to check the integrity of already downloaded files, which is potentially quite useful.

There are a couple of options regarding this feature:
* Add a separate method for this (`checksum()`?) functionality. Its input could be a list of product ids and a directory or a list of file paths.
* Not implement this at all. The zip files can be verified quite easily with `zip -T` or `7z t` commands or via a GUI.
* Re-add it to `download()`. Since you are likely interested in fixing the broken files rather than just deleting them, `download()` is actually a quite sensible place for this functionality.

The consensus so far in #126 was to make it a separate function.

issue_title: Segmentation fault on ONNX 1.2.2 from pypi on python3
Squeeze operator attribute "axes" should be optional.
issue_body:
Currently squeeze operator requires attribute "axis"  attribute .(https://github.com/onnx/onnx/blob/master/docs/Operators.md#Squeeze)

I think this attribute should be marked optional. As per convention on most frameworks( i.e TF, Pytorch, MXNet), absence of this attribute meant all the dimensions with value "1" will be removed.

This will be useful  for converters while exporting models to ONNX when "axis" is not specified, else the input shape has to be determined. Also, will be in accordance with numpy squeeze definition

@bddppq @houseroad @dzhulgakov
Probably https://github.com/onnx/onnx/blob/master/docs/Installation.md should be removed, because it's outdated and the README.md already has installation instructions.
